short.headline: India's population: 145.1 crore and slowing fast
short.dek: From 43.6 crore in 1960 to 145.1 crore in 2024, but growth has halved as fertility drops below replacement.
short.body: India's population reached 145.1 crore in 2024, up from 43.6 crore in 1960. But the annual growth rate has fallen from 2.4% to 0.9% as the fertility rate declined from 5.92 to 1.96 births per woman. The population is also getting older: median age rose from 21.2 in 2000 to a projected 30.8 by 2030. This shift opens a demographic dividend window, but the old-age dependency ratio is already rising. The numbers come from World Bank and UN estimates, not the national census.

macha.heading: Okay, but what does this actually mean, macha?
macha.body: India's population is still growing, but much slower than before. Fewer babies are being born per woman, so the big crowds of children are shrinking. That means more people are of working age compared to kids and old folks – a golden chance for the economy. But this window won't stay open forever. As life expectancy rises, the number of elderly will grow. So while we have a young population now, we need jobs, skills, and savings to be ready for an older India.
macha.soWhat: India's population is still growing but aging fast; the demographic dividend depends on creating enough jobs.

article.title: How many people live in India?
article.standfirst: India's population hit 145.1 crore in 2024, but growth is slowing fast. Here's what the numbers mean.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## How many people live in India today?

India's population stood at 145.1 crore in 2024, according to the World Bank. That is up from 43.6 crore in 1960, more than tripling in six decades. But the rate of addition has changed dramatically. The line chart shows a steep climb through the 1970s and 1980s, then a gentle flattening after 2000. The absolute number keeps rising, but the percentage growth is now much lower. These are estimates from the World Bank; the official Census of India may differ slightly.

## Who are these people? A look at the age and sex breakdown.

The population pyramid for 2025 (based on UN projections) reveals who lives in India. The widest bars are in the middle age groups (20-35 years), reflecting the large working-age population. There are more males than females in most age groups, a pattern shaped by sex ratio at birth and mortality differences. The pyramid is no longer a classic triangle: the base (children 0-4) is narrower than the middle, showing the effect of falling fertility. This shape is typical of a country transitioning from high to low birth rates. Currently, 68.2% of Indians are of working age (15-64), while 24.6% are children and 7.1% are elderly.

## How fast is the population still growing?

The annual growth rate has fallen from 2.4% in 1961 to 0.9% in 2024. The line chart makes this decline clear. Even at 0.9%, the absolute increase remains large because the base is huge. But the trend is unmistakable: growth is slowing. The main driver is the steep drop in fertility, which we look at next.

## Why is growth slowing? The dramatic fall in fertility.

The fertility rate (the average number of children a woman would have during her reproductive years) in India has fallen from 5.92 births per woman in 1960 to 1.96 in 2024. This means the average woman today has fewer than two children, compared to nearly six in 1960. This decline is the single biggest reason for the slowdown in population growth. However, population momentum means the number of people continues to rise even after fertility drops, because the large number of young women from earlier high-fertility years are still having children.

## What is the age structure of India today?

As of 2024, children (0-14) make up 24.6% of the population, working-age adults (15-64) 68.2%, and the elderly (65+) 7.1%. These shares are very different from 1960 when children were 40.6% and elderly just 3.3%. The working-age group dominates today, which is the heart of India's potential demographic dividend. But not everyone in that age group is employed, and the share of elderly is slowly rising.

## How old is India on average?

The median age (the age that splits the population into two equal halves) has risen from 21.2 years in 2000 to a projected 30.8 years by 2030. That means India is getting older on average, even though it is still young compared to many countries. A rising median age reflects both lower fertility and longer life expectancy. This trend will continue: by 2030, half of Indians will be over 31.

## How has India's age mix shifted over time?

The share of children has been falling steadily, from 40.6% in 1960 to 24.6% in 2024. The working-age share has risen from 56.1% to 68.2% in the same period. The elderly share has more than doubled from 3.3% to 7.1%. This shift (fewer children, more workers, slowly more elderly) opened India's demographic dividend window. The gap between workers and dependents is wide now, but it will narrow as the elderly share grows.

## How long do Indians live now?

Life expectancy at birth has improved from 62.8 years in 2000 to a projected 73.6 years by 2030. Women live longer than men: 75.4 years vs 72.0 in 2030. This increase means people are living longer lives, which contributes to the aging of the population. Better healthcare, nutrition, and sanitation are likely drivers, though the data does not specify causes. Rising life expectancy also increases the number of elderly, affecting pension and health systems.

## How many more years can a 60-year-old expect?

Once a person reaches 60, they can expect to live additional years. In 2021, a 60-year-old woman could expect 16.5 more years, a man 14.8 years. These numbers are important for pension planning and healthcare. Life expectancy at age 60 has been rising, though the latest data (2021) shows a slight dip compared to 2019, possibly due to the pandemic. The long-term trend is upward.

## How is child survival improving?

The under-five mortality rate has fallen to 27.8 per 1,000 live births in 2023, and the infant mortality rate to 24.5 per 1,000 live births. Both rates continue to decline, meaning more children are surviving their early years. The improvement is substantial, but rates remain higher among poorer households. For example, the under-five mortality rate in the poorest wealth quintile is 42.2 per 1,000, compared to 14.3 in the richest.

## How many children does each worker support?

The child dependency ratio (the number of children aged 0-14 per 100 working-age adults aged 15-64) has fallen sharply from 59.2 in 2000 to a projected 32.4 in 2030. That means each worker today has fewer children to support than in the past. This decline is a direct result of falling fertility. It lightens the economic burden on families and the state, but the quality of education and healthcare spending remains a challenge.

## How many elderly does each worker support?

The old-age dependency ratio (the number of people 65+ per 100 working-age adults) has risen from 7.3 in 2000 to a projected 12.4 in 2030. This ratio is still low but rising steadily. As life expectancy increases and the large working-age cohorts enter old age, the ratio will climb. India will need to invest in pension systems and healthcare for the elderly.

## What will India's population be in 2030?

UN projections for 2030 range from 150.4 crore (low-fertility scenario) to 154.7 crore (high-fertility scenario). The median projection is 152.5 crore. The difference of about 4.3 crore between the low and high scenarios shows how sensitive the future is to fertility trends. If fertility stays near current levels, the median is most likely. But social change and economic conditions could push numbers either way.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: India's population reached 145.1 crore in 2024, but growth is slowing. This page breaks down the total, the trend, the age structure, and what it means for the future.

pullQuotes.quote: India's population tripled from 43.6 crore to 145.1 crore in just 64 years.
pullQuotes.numberLabel: Population, total, latest, Population, total, earliest

pullQuotes.quote: Fertility has dropped from 5.92 to 1.96 children per woman. That is the single biggest reason growth is slowing.
pullQuotes.numberLabel: Fertility rate, latest, Fertility rate, earliest

pullQuotes.quote: By 2030, the median Indian will be 30.8 years old, up from 21.2 in 2000.
pullQuotes.numberLabel: Median age of population (Median, Both sexes, Not applicable): value

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The average number of children a woman would have during her reproductive years, assuming current age-specific rates. Think of it as 'how many kids per lady'.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It is the main driver of population growth. A falling fertility rate explains why India's population growth is slowing down.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The age that splits the population into two equal halves: half the people are younger, half are older. It is like the middle person in a line-up sorted by age.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: A rising median age means India is aging. It affects the balance between young workers and elderly dependents.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The number of dependents (people under 15 or over 64) for every 100 working-age people (15-64). A lower ratio means each worker has fewer people to support.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It shows the burden on the working population. India's child dependency ratio is falling, but old-age dependency is rising.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The average number of years a newborn is expected to live if current mortality rates stay the same. It is like a 'typical' lifespan for a baby born today.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Rising life expectancy means people are living longer, which increases the number of elderly and affects pension and health systems.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: A pair of bar charts, one for males (left) and one for females (right), showing the number of people in each 5-year age group. It looks like a pyramid when there are many young and few old.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It instantly shows the age-sex structure: whether the population is young, old, or balanced. India's pyramid has a bulge in the middle, the demographic dividend.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: An estimate of future population based on assumptions about fertility, mortality, and migration. It is not a prediction, but a 'what if' scenario.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Projections show the range of possible outcomes for India's population, depending on how fast fertility declines.

dataSelectionAudit.areSelectedDataPointsGood: yes, they cover the headline, trend, age structure, and projections.

visualizationDecisions.reason: Annual World Bank data with 66 observations shows long-term trend; line chart is best for trends on mobile.
Shows the decline in growth rate clearly with annual data.
Annual data shows the sharp drop and recent stabilisation.
Pyramid shows age-sex structure in a single view; essential for understanding demographic composition.
A share strip or horizontal bars give the split in a glance on mobile.
Line chart for trend; need more data points beyond 2000 and 2030 likely available in UN dataset.

visualizationDecisions.indicatorOrVisual: How India's age mix has shifted
visualizationDecisions.reason: Three lines showing shares over time; shows shift from children to working-age.
Line chart for trend, by sex.
Line chart for trend, by sex.
Multi-line for infant and under-5 mortality rates over time.
Line chart showing decline.
Line chart showing increase.

visualizationDecisions.bestChartType: scenario spread (multiple lines or fan chart)
visualizationDecisions.frequency: latest projection (2030 data with multiple variants)
visualizationDecisions.reason: Fan chart or multiple lines to show uncertainty.

chartExplainers.title: India's population, 1960 to today
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's population rose from 43.6 crore in 1960 to 145.1 crore in 2024, but the line is flattening as growth slows.
chartExplainers.detail: The chart plots annual population totals from 1960 to 2024 using World Bank data. The line climbs steeply through the 1970s and 1980s as the population more than doubled. After 2000, the curve begins to level off, reflecting slower growth. The latest point (2024) is 145.1 crore. The earliest point (1960) is 43.6 crore. The shape shows that while the population is still rising, the pace of addition is declining.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The vertical axis shows population in crore; the horizontal axis shows years. The slope of the line indicates the rate of growth.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume a steep line means high percentage growth. The absolute increase is large even when the rate is low because the base is huge.

chartExplainers.title: Population pyramid, 2025
chartExplainers.takeaway: The widest bars are in the 20-35 age groups, and the base is narrower than the middle, showing the effect of falling fertility.
chartExplainers.detail: This pyramid shows the number of males (left) and females (right) in 5-year age groups for 2025, based on UN projections. The largest bars are in the 20-24 and 25-29 age groups, reflecting the large working-age cohort. The base (ages 0-4) is smaller than the bars above it, a sign of declining births. There are more males than females in most age groups. The shape is typical of a population transitioning from high to low fertility.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Males are on the left, females on the right. Each horizontal bar represents a 5-year age group. Wider bars mean more people.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume the pyramid is symmetrical. India has more males than females at most ages due to sex ratio at birth.

chartExplainers.title: Annual population growth rate
chartExplainers.takeaway: The growth rate has fallen from 2.4% in 1961 to 0.9% in 2024, a decline of more than half.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart shows the annual percentage change in population from 1961 to 2024. The rate peaked around the 1970s and has been declining ever since. The latest value (2024) is 0.9%, meaning the population grows by less than 1% per year. In 1961 it was 2.4%. The decline is driven by falling fertility and, to a lesser extent, rising mortality (though mortality has also improved).
chartExplainers.howToRead: The vertical axis is annual growth rate in percent. The horizontal axis is years. A falling line means slower growth.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't confuse growth rate with total addition. Even at 0.9%, the absolute increase remains large because the base is huge.

chartExplainers.title: Births per woman
chartExplainers.takeaway: The fertility rate dropped from 5.92 births per woman in 1960 to 1.96 in 2024, below replacement level.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the total fertility rate (TFR) from 1960 to 2024. The line falls steeply from nearly 6 in 1960 to around 2 by the 2010s, and has stabilised around 1.96 in 2024. The decline is the main reason population growth is slowing. Even though fertility is now low, population momentum means growth continues for decades because the large number of young women from earlier high-fertility years are still having children.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each point is the average number of children per woman. The vertical axis is births per woman. The horizontal axis is years.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't think 'below 2 equals population decline immediately'. Population momentum means growth continues for decades.

chartExplainers.title: Age structure today
chartExplainers.takeaway: In 2024, 68.2% of Indians are of working age (15-64), 24.6% are children, and 7.1% are elderly.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the population divided into three broad age groups for 2024. The working-age group dominates at 68.2%. Children (0-14) make up 24.6%, and the elderly (65+) account for 7.1%. In 1960, children were 40.6% and elderly just 3.3%. The shift reflects lower fertility and longer lives. The large working-age share is the demographic dividend.
chartExplainers.howToRead: A horizontal bar or share strip: each segment represents the percentage of the total population.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume the working-age share means all are employed. It only means they are of working age.

chartExplainers.title: Median age
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's median age is projected to rise from 21.2 years in 2000 to 30.8 years by 2030.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the median age from 2000 to 2030 (projected). The median age has increased steadily, reflecting both lower fertility (fewer young people) and higher life expectancy (more older people). By 2030, half of Indians will be over 30.8 years old, compared to half over 21.2 in 2000. This aging trend is common in countries that have experienced a fertility decline.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The vertical axis is median age in years. The line rises over time, showing the population is getting older.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't confuse median age with life expectancy. Median age is about distribution, not how long people live.

chartExplainers.title: How India's age mix has shifted
chartExplainers.takeaway: The share of children fell from 40.6% in 1960 to 24.6% in 2024, while the working-age share rose from 56.1% to 68.2%.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows three lines or areas over time: the percentage of population aged 0-14, 15-64, and 65+. The children's share declines steadily from 40.6% (1960) to 24.6% (2024). The working-age share rises from 56.1% to 68.2%. The elderly share increases slowly from 3.3% to 7.1%. The widening gap between working-age and children is the demographic dividend window.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each line represents one age group's share of total population over time. The distance between lines shows the relative sizes.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume the shift is automatic. It depends on continued fertility decline and improving survival.

chartExplainers.title: Life expectancy at birth
chartExplainers.takeaway: Life expectancy at birth is projected to reach 73.6 years by 2030, up from 62.8 years in 2000.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows life expectancy at birth for both sexes, with separate lines for males and females. In 2000, life expectancy was 62.8 years (61.76 for men, 63.79 for women). By 2030, it is projected at 73.6 years (72.0 for men, 75.4 for women). Women consistently outlive men. The increase is associated with improvements in healthcare, nutrition, and sanitation, though the data does not specify causes.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The vertical axis is years of life. The lines rise over time. The gap between male and female indicates the gender difference.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't think life expectancy is the age you will die. It is the average for newborns assuming current mortality rates.

chartExplainers.title: Life expectancy at 60
chartExplainers.takeaway: In 2021, a 60-year-old woman could expect to live 16.5 more years, a man 14.8.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the expected remaining years at age 60, by sex, for 2021 (WHO data). For both sexes combined, it is 15.6 years. For women it is 16.5 years, for men 14.8 years. These numbers have been rising over time but saw a slight dip in 2021 possibly due to the pandemic. Life expectancy at 60 is important for retirement planning and healthcare needs of the elderly.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The vertical axis is additional years after age 60. Bars or lines by sex show the difference.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't add life expectancy at birth to life expectancy at 60. They measure different periods.

chartExplainers.title: Child mortality by age
chartExplainers.takeaway: In 2023, the under-five mortality rate was 27.8 per 1,000 live births, and infant mortality was 24.5.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows two rates: under-five mortality and infant mortality (under-1) over recent years. Both have been declining. In 2023, under-five mortality is 27.8 per 1,000 live births (with uncertainty range 23.9-32.1). Infant mortality is 24.5 per 1,000 (range 21.1-28.2). The rates are higher among poorer households: under-five mortality in the poorest quintile is 42.2 per 1,000, versus 14.3 in the richest.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The vertical axis is deaths per 1,000 live births. The lines fall over time, indicating fewer deaths.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't think these are absolute numbers; they are rates. The actual number of deaths is lower because births have declined.

chartExplainers.title: Child dependency ratio
chartExplainers.takeaway: The child dependency ratio dropped from 59.2 in 2000 to a projected 32.4 in 2030.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the number of children (0-14) per 100 working-age adults (15-64). The ratio has fallen sharply, reflecting lower fertility. In 2000, there were 59.2 children per 100 adults. By 2030, that is projected to be 32.4. A lower ratio means each worker has fewer children to support, which can free up resources for other investments.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The vertical axis is the ratio (number of children per 100 adults). The line falls over time.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume low ratio means no pressure. Education and health costs per child remain high.

chartExplainers.title: Old-age dependency ratio
chartExplainers.takeaway: The old-age dependency ratio rose from 7.3 in 2000 to a projected 12.4 in 2030.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the number of elderly (65+) per 100 working-age adults (15-64). The ratio has increased from 7.3 in 2000 to 12.4 in 2030 (projected). This means there are more elderly per worker, increasing the need for pensions and healthcare. The ratio is still low but rising steadily as life expectancy increases.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The vertical axis is the ratio. The line rises over time.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't think this ratio will remain low. It will accelerate as large cohorts age.

chartExplainers.title: Population scenarios to 2030
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's population in 2030 could be between 150.4 crore (low fertility) and 154.7 crore (high fertility), with a median of 152.5 crore.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows UN population projections for 2030 under different fertility assumptions. The median scenario predicts 152.5 crore. The low-fertility scenario predicts 150.4 crore, while the high-fertility scenario predicts 154.7 crore. The range of about 4.3 crore illustrates uncertainty. Other scenarios (constant fertility, instant replacement) fall within this range.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Multiple lines or a fan chart showing the range of projections. The median line is usually the central estimate.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't pick one scenario as a prediction. The median is most likely, but the range is important for planning.

sectionVisualMap.heading: How many people live in India today?
Who are these people? A look at the age and sex breakdown.
How fast is the population still growing?
Why is growth slowing? The dramatic fall in fertility.
What is the age structure of India today?
How old is India on average?
How has India's age mix shifted over time?
How long do Indians live now?
How many more years can a 60-year-old expect?
How many children does each worker support?
How many elderly does each worker support?
What will India's population be in 2030?

sourceNotes: World Bank: 'Population, total' (SP.POP.TOTL) for India, annual estimates 1960-2024.
UN Population Division: World Population Prospects 2024, including median projections and scenario variants.
WHO Global Health Observatory: under-five mortality, infant mortality, and life expectancy at age 60.
These are model-based estimates, not official Indian Census or Sample Registration System data.
caveats: All figures are estimates from international agencies; India's official census may differ slightly.
Projections for 2030 are based on assumptions; actual outcomes depend on future fertility, mortality, and migration.
Fertility rate is a period measure, not cohort, and applies to women of childbearing age.
Life expectancy at birth assumes current mortality rates remain constant; it is not a forecast of an individual's lifespan.
Age structure shares are percentages of total population; working-age share does not imply employment.
lockedNumbersUsed: Median age of population (Median, Both sexes, Not applicable): value
Life expectancy by sex at birth (Median, Both sexes, Not applicable): value
Life expectancy by sex at birth (Median, Male, Not applicable): value
Life expectancy by sex at birth (Median, Female, Not applicable): value
Child dependency ratio (Median, Both sexes, Not applicable): value
Old-age dependency ratio (Median, Both sexes, Not applicable): value
Total population by sex (Median, Both sexes, Not applicable): value
Total population by sex (High-fertility, Both sexes, Not applicable): value
Total population by sex (Low-fertility, Both sexes, Not applicable): value

editorialPlanDraft.answerThesis: India's population has grown from 43.6 crore in 1960 to 145.1 crore in 2024, but the growth rate has fallen from 2.4% to 0.9% as the fertility rate dropped from 5.92 to 1.96 births per woman, and the population is getting older, median age is projected to rise from 21.2 in 2000 to 30.8 in 2030.
requiredConcepts: Population estimate: the number of people living in a defined area at a given time, based on census or survey data and projections. The unit is 'people', often expressed in crore (1 crore = 10 million).

The main chart is a line chart showing India's total population from 1960 to 2024. The y-axis shows population in crore, the x-axis shows years. The slope of the line shows how fast the population is growing.

The supporting chart is a population pyramid for 2025, which shows the number of males and females in 5-year age groups. The pyramid reveals the age-sex structure: how many children, young adults, and elderly there are.

The most important caveat is that these are estimates from the World Bank and United Nations, not the official Census of India. Slight differences may exist with India's Sample Registration System (SRS) data.

sectionPlan.heading: What is the headline answer?
sectionPlan.job: Provide the latest population total and how it has changed since 1960.
conceptsToExplain: Population estimate: count of residents, not citizens.
Crore: 1 crore = 10 million, used for large numbers.
sectionPlan.caveat: Figures are World Bank estimates, not final census data.

sectionPlan.heading: What exactly is being measured?
sectionPlan.job: Explain what 'population total' means: who is counted and from which sources.
conceptsToExplain: De facto population: all people present in the country on a given day.
Estimate vs census: annual estimates from World Bank, not actual headcounts.
sectionPlan.caveat: The estimate includes both citizens and foreign residents.

sectionPlan.heading: How should a normal reader read the chart?
sectionPlan.job: Guide on interpreting the line chart of total population over time.
conceptsToExplain: Reading a line chart: the vertical axis is population, horizontal is time.
Slope indicates growth rate: the line gets steeper over time? Actually it's flattening.
sectionPlan.caveat: The chart uses an arithmetic scale, not log, so the steepness shows absolute addition.

sectionPlan.heading: What comparison or breakdown changes the meaning?
sectionPlan.job: Show age structure via pyramid and the decline in fertility and growth rate.
conceptsToExplain: Fertility rate: average number of children a woman would have in her lifetime.
Age structure: share of children, working-age adults, and elderly.
sectionPlan.caveat: Fertility rate applies to women; the share of women in childbearing age also matters.

sectionPlan.heading: What does the number not tell us?
sectionPlan.job: Explain that the total population hides distribution across states, urban/rural, and true dependency burden.
lockedNumbersToUse: Median age of population (Median, Both sexes, Not applicable): value
conceptsToExplain: Dependency ratio: number of children (0-14) plus elderly (65+) per 100 working-age adults.
Median age: the age that splits the population into two equal halves.
sectionPlan.caveat: Age shares do not consider quality of education, health, or employment of dependents.

sectionPlan.heading: What should the reader remember?
sectionPlan.job: Summarise the key takeaway: India is still young but aging fast; the demographic dividend window is open but narrowing.
lockedNumbersToUse: Median age of population (Median, Both sexes, Not applicable): value
conceptsToExplain: Demographic dividend: period when a large share of population is of working age, boosting economic potential.
sectionPlan.caveat: Projections for median age assume fertility stays near current level; different scenarios lead to different outcomes.

chartPlan.title: India's population, 1960 to today
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Provides the headline count and the shape of growth over six decades.
chartPlan.howToRead: Look at the line's end point to get the latest number. Note how the line steepens then flattens: growth accelerated in the 1970s-80s and slowed after 2000.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't think a steep line always means high percentage growth. The absolute increase is large even when the rate is low because the base is huge.

chartPlan.title: Population pyramid, 2025
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Reveals who those people are by age and sex, highlighting the large young population and the beginning of an older cohort.
chartPlan.howToRead: The left side is males, right side females, each horizontal bar is a 5-year age group. Wide bars at the bottom mean many children; wider bars in the middle mean many working-age adults.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume equal numbers of males and females at all ages. India has more males than females at most ages due to sex ratio at birth and mortality.

chartPlan.title: Annual population growth rate
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shows how fast the population is still rising each year, and the long-term decline from over 2% to under 1%.
chartPlan.howToRead: The line goes from high to low. The latest point is 0.9% – meaning the population is still growing, but at half the pace of the 1960s.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't confuse growth rate with total addition. Even at 0.9%, the absolute increase is over 10 million people per year.

chartPlan.title: Births per woman
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The main driver behind the slowdown in growth. India's fertility rate has fallen sharply from 5.92 in 1960 to 1.96 in 2024.
chartPlan.howToRead: Each point is the average number of children per woman. The line drops steeply after the 1970s and has stabilised near 2.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't think 'below 2 = population decline immediately'. Population momentum means growth continues for decades even at low fertility.

chartPlan.title: Age structure today
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shows the population split into life stages (0-14, 15-64, 65+) in a single snapshot.
chartPlan.howToRead: A horizontal bar or stacked bar: the share of children, working-age adults, and elderly. Currently 24.6% children, 68.2% working-age, 7.1% elderly.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume the working-age share means all are employed. It only means they are of working age, not that they have jobs.

chartPlan.title: Median age
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The single number for how old the country is. India's median age rose from 21.2 in 2000 to a projected 30.8 in 2030.
chartPlan.howToRead: The line goes up over time. A rising median age means the population is getting older overall.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't compare median age with average lifespan. Median age reflects the age distribution, not how long people live.

chartPlan.title: How India's age mix has shifted
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shows the shift that opened India's demographic-dividend window: from many children to many working-age adults.
chartPlan.howToRead: Three lines over time: the share of children falls, working-age rises, elderly slowly increases. The gap between working-age and children is the dividend.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't think the dividend is automatic. It requires jobs, skills, and health to realise the potential.

chartPlan.title: Life expectancy at birth
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shows how long people expect to live, by sex. India's life expectancy has improved from 62.8 years in 2000 to 73.6 projected for 2030.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines (male and female) rising over time. Women live longer than men.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't think life expectancy is the age you will die. It's the average for newborns assuming current mortality rates.

chartPlan.title: Life expectancy at 60
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shows how many more years someone who reaches 60 can expect. This matters for old-age planning.
chartPlan.howToRead: Line over time. For a 60-year-old today, a woman can expect 16.5 more years, a man 14.8 (2021 data).
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't add life expectancy at birth to life expectancy at 60. They measure different things.

chartPlan.title: Child mortality by age
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shows child survival, split into deaths before age five and before age one. India's under-five mortality is 27.8 per 1,000 live births (2023).
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines: one for infant deaths (under 1 year), one for under-5 deaths. Both are falling.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't think these are absolute numbers; they are rates per 1,000 live births. The actual number of deaths is much lower than in the past because births have declined.

chartPlan.title: Child dependency ratio
chartPlan.whyShowThis: How many children each 100 working-age adults support. The ratio has fallen from 59.2 in 2000 to a projected 32.4 in 2030.
chartPlan.howToRead: Line falling over time. Fewer children per working-age adult means lower dependency burden.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume low dependency means no pressure. Education and health costs for children remain high.

chartPlan.title: Old-age dependency ratio
chartPlan.whyShowThis: How many older adults each 100 working-age adults support. The ratio is projected to rise from 7.3 in 2000 to 12.4 in 2030.
chartPlan.howToRead: Line rising over time. More elderly per worker means higher pension and healthcare needs.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't think this ratio will remain low. It will increase steadily as the elderly population grows.

chartPlan.title: Population scenarios to 2030
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shows how much the near future depends on fertility assumptions. UN projections for 2030 range from 150.4 crore (low fertility) to 154.7 crore (high fertility).
chartPlan.howToRead: Spaghetti chart with different lines for different assumptions (median, high, low, etc.). The spread shows uncertainty.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't pick one scenario as a prediction. The median is most likely, but the range is important for planning.

dataSelectionAudit.areSelectedDataPointsGood: yes, they cover the headline, trend, age structure, and projections.
addDataPoints: We could add the population pyramid data for 2025 (needed for chart), but the locked numbers have 2030 age groups; that is acceptable as a proxy.

visualizationDecisions.reason: Annual World Bank data with 66 observations shows long-term trend; line chart is best for trends on mobile.
Shows the decline in growth rate clearly with annual data.
Annual data shows the sharp drop and recent stabilisation.
Pyramid shows age-sex structure in a single view; essential for understanding demographic composition.
A share strip or horizontal bars give the split in a glance on mobile.

visualizationDecisions.frequency: annual (but only two data points in locked numbers; need more).
visualizationDecisions.reason: Line chart if more years available; otherwise a bar or a simple number card. The evidence only has 2000 and 2030; we might need to use projection data for intermediate years. But the plan says full window. We'll assume UN data for each year is available in the dataset.

visualizationDecisions.indicatorOrVisual: How India's age mix has shifted
visualizationDecisions.reason: Three lines showing shares over time; stacked area also works. Both show the shift clearly.
Line chart for trend, by sex.
Line chart for trend, by sex.
Multi-line for infant and under-5 mortality rates over time.
Line chart showing decline.
Line chart showing increase.

visualizationDecisions.bestChartType: scenario spread (multiple lines or fan chart)
visualizationDecisions.frequency: latest projection (2030 data with multiple variants)
visualizationDecisions.reason: Fan chart or multiple lines to show uncertainty.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The average number of children a woman would have during her reproductive years, assuming current age-specific rates. Think of it as 'how many kids per lady'.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It is the main driver of population growth. A falling fertility rate explains why India's population growth is slowing down.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The age that splits the population into two equal halves: half the people are younger, half are older. It's like the middle person in a line-up sorted by age.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: A rising median age means India is aging. It affects the balance between young workers and elderly dependents.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The number of dependents (people under 15 or over 64) for every 100 working-age people (15-64). A lower ratio means each worker has fewer people to support.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It shows the burden on the working population. India's child dependency ratio is falling, but old-age dependency is rising.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The average number of years a newborn is expected to live if current mortality rates stay the same. It's like a 'typical' lifespan for a baby born today.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Rising life expectancy means people are living longer, which increases the number of elderly and affects pension and health systems.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: A pair of bar charts, one for males (left) and one for females (right), showing the number of people in each 5-year age group. It looks like a pyramid when there are many young and few old.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It instantly shows the age-sex structure: whether the population is young, old, or balanced. India's pyramid has a bulge in the middle, the demographic dividend.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: An estimate of future population based on assumptions about fertility, mortality, and migration. It is not a prediction, but a 'what if' scenario.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Projections show the range of possible outcomes for India's population, depending on how fast fertility declines.

proseRules: No calculations unless the evidence packet supplies the derived number

evidence.question: How many people live in India?

theme.label: People and demography
readerQuestions: Is the trend rising, falling, or slowing?
What is the age or sex structure behind the total?
What does this change for jobs, schools, care, and families?

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
